content.full_text:
FAIR p OLITICAL p RACTICES COMMISSION 
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento. CA 958 I 4-2329 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
Julie Pierce 
1028 Tiffin Drive 
Cla1ton, CA 94517 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our file No. A-11-141 
Dear Ms. Pierce: 
August 26, 2011 
This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the conflict-of-interest 
provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act"). 1 We base our advice on the facts presented; 
the Fair Political Practices Commission (the "Commission") does not act as a finder of fact when 
it renders advice. (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.) Additionally, we base our advice 
solely on the provisions of the Act and do not address the applicability, if any, of other conflict­
of-interest laws such as common law conflict of interest. One of your questions is purely 
hypothetical and, therefore, we decline to render advice or informal assistance on this question at 
this time. (Regulation 18329(b)(8)(D), copy enclosed.) If the situation changes so that the 
question is no longer hypothetical, you may write to the Commission for further advice. 
QUESTION 
Do you have a disqualifying conflict of interest that would prohibit you from making or 
participating in making city council decisions regarding a proposed project based on (1) your 
position as a co-trustee and sole beneficiary of a revocable trust that owns a house located more 
than 500 feet from the project boundaries, or (2) your occupancy of the house? 
CONCLUSION 
No. A public official who is a beneficiary of a trust does not have an economic interest 
in the trust's real property if the official does not currently receive income from the trust and 
does not have an irrevocable future right to receive income or principal. Occupancy alone, does 
not give rise to an interest in real property. 
1 The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory 
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 


FACTS 
File No. A-11-141 
Page No. 2 
You are a city council member for the City of Clayton. The city council will be making 
decisions regarding a land use application filed by the Clayton Community Church for a project 
in the city's downtown area. The application includes a proposal to use existing on-street public 
parking spaces as well as off-street public and private parking lots in and around the downtown 
area to satisfy the city's on-site parking requirements. The outlying off-site parking areas are 
considered to be within the boundaries of the project. Various elements of the application will 
require changes to Clayton's General Plan, Town Center Specific Plan and zoning ordinance. 
You reside in a house located more than 500 feet from downtown and the proposed 
project. The house is owned by a revocable trust in which you are a co-trustee and the sole 
beneficiary. Your father is the trustor, as well as the other co-trustee. You state that you have no 
other interest in the real property. Your name does not appear on the title of the property. 
However, you are included as a responsible party on the mortgage. You do not receive any 
income from the trust 
ANALYSIS 
The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions ensure that public officials will "perform their 
duties in an impartial manner, free from bias caused by their own financial interests or the 
financial interests of persons who have supported them." (Section 81001(b).) Section 87100 
prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or otherwise using his or her 
official position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a financial 
interest. 
The Commission has adopted an eight-step standard analysis for deciding whether an 
official has a disqualifying conflict of interest. (Regulation 18700(b).) The general rule, 
however, is that a conflict of interest exists whenever a public official makes a governmental 
decision that has a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on one or more of his or her 
financial interests. 
STEPS 1 & 2: ARE YOU A PuBuc OFFICIAL MAKING, PARTICIPATING IN MAKING, OR 
INFLUENCING A GOVERNMENTAL DECISION? 
As a member of the city council, you are a public official under the Act. (Section 82048.) 
Consequently, you may not make, participate in making, or otherwise use your official position to 
influence any decisions that will have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on any of 
your economic interests. Because you will be called upon to' consider and vote to approve the 
church's application, you will be making and participating in making a governmental decision.2 
2 If a public official's office is listed in section 87200 ("87200 filers" include planning commission 
members) and he or she has a conflict of interest in a decision noticed at a public meeting, then he or she must: ( l) 
immediately prior to the discussion of the item, verbally identify each type of economic interest involved in the 
decision as well as details of the economic interest, as discussed in regulation 18702.S(b)(l)(B), on the record of the 


File No. A-11-141 
Page No. 3 
STEP 3: Do You HA VE A POTENTIALLY DISQUALIFYING ECONOMIC INTEREST? 
A public official has a financial interest in a decision within the meaning of Section 
87103 if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect, 
distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on the official, a member of his or her 
immediate family, or on any of the official's economic interests. The only economic interest 
implicated by your facts is an economic interest in real property. 
Section 82033 of the Act defines an interest in real property as: 
"Interest in real property" includes any leasehold, beneficial or ownership 
interest or an option to acquire such an interest in real property located in 
the jurisdiction owned directly, indirectly or beneficially by the public 
official, or other filer, or his or her immediate family if the fair market 
value of the interest is two thousand dollars ($2,000) or more. Interests in 
real property of an individual includes a pro rata share of interests in real 
property of any business entity or trust in which the individual or 
immediate family owns, directly, indirectly or beneficially, a IO-percent 
interest or greater." 
An official has an economic interest in the real property held by a trust when the official 
has a direct, indirect, or beneficial interest of 10 percent or greater in the trust. (Regulation 
18234( a).) An official has a direct, indirect, or beneficial interest in a trust if the official is: 
"(1) A trustor and: 
"(A) Can revoke or terminate the trust; 
"(B) Has retained or reserved any rights to the income or principal of the trust, 
or retained any reversionary or remainder interest; or 
"(C) Has retained or reserved any power of appointment, including but not 
limited to the power to change the trustee, or the power to amend, alter or 
designate, either alone or in conjunction with anyone else, the person or 
persons who shall possess or enjoy the trust property or income. 
"(2) A beneficiary and: 
"(A) Presently receives income; or 
"(B) Has an irrevocable future right to receive income or principal. For 
purposes of this subsection, an individual has an irrevocable future right to 
receive income or principal if the trust is irrevocable, unless one of the 
following applies: 
1"(i) Powers exist to consume, invade, or appoint the principal for the 
benefit of beneficiaries other than the official and such powers are not limited 
meeting; (2) recuse himself or herself; and (3) leave the room for the duration of the discussion and/or vote on the 
item. For closed sessions, consent calendars, absences and speaking as a member of the public regarding personal 
interests, special rules found in regulation 18702.5, subdivisions (c) and (d) apply. (Section 87105.) 


File No. A-11-141 
Page No. 4 
by an ascertainable standard relating to the health, education, support, or 
maintenance of the beneficiaries; or 
"(ii) Under the terms of the trust, someone other than the official can 
designate the persons who shall possess or enjoy the trust property or 
income." (Regulation 18234(b )-( c ). ) 
Under Regulation 18234(c), a public official has an economic interest in real property 
held in a trust when the official is either the trustor or a beneficiary of the trust that meets certain 
conditions. Because you are not the trustor, we tum to Regulation 18234(c)(2) to determine 
whether you meet the conditions, as a beneficiary, that would establish your economic interest in 
the trust's real property. 
A beneficiary has an economic interest in real property held in a trust if the official 
currently receives income from the trust or has an irrevocable future right to receive income or 
principal from the trust. You do not currently receive income from the trust and, under the terms 
of a revocable trust, you do not have an irrevocable right to future income or principal. 
Accordingly, you do not meet the conditions that would establish an economic interest in real 
property based on the positions you hold in the trust. 
However, you may have an economic interest in the trust's real property if you occupy 
the house under a lease. Under Section 82003, an "interest in real property" includes any 
leasehold, beneficial, or ownership interest in, or an option to acquire such an interest in, real 
property located in the jurisdiction of the public official if the fair market value of the interest is 
$2,000 or more. However, under the facts, your right of occupancy is not based on a lease, but 
rather, on agreement by the co-trustees to allow you to occupy the premises. Under these 
circumstances, you do not have an interest in real property based on a lease. 
Because you do not have an economic interest in real property, you may make, 
participate in making or use your official position to influence city council decisions regarding 
the church's application without violating the Act's conflict-of-interest provisions. 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
Sincerely, 
Zackery P. Morazzini 
General Counsel 
-r 
I 
By: 
Valentina Joyce 
Counsel, Legal Division 
VJ:jgl 
Enclosure

No. A public official who is a beneficiary of a trust does not have an economic interest 
in the trust's real property if the official does not currently receive income from the trust and 
does not have an irrevocable future right to receive income or principal. Occupancy alone, does 
not give rise to an interest in real property.

participating in making city council decisions regarding a proposed project based on (1) your 
position as a co-trustee and sole beneficiary of a revocable trust that owns a house located more 
than 500 feet from the project boundaries, or (2) your occupancy of the house?

CONCLUSION: No. A public official who is a beneficiary of a trust does not have an economic interest 
in the trust's real property if the official does not currently receive income from the trust and 
does not have an irrevocable future right to receive income or principal. Occupancy alone, does 
not give rise to an interest in real property.
